context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk tbh PS6 is frying me rn, maybe the graph coloring stuff? I’m confused like super generally, can u just explain the whole idea first?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, PS6 can be tough! But rather than me explaining everything upfront, let's dig in together — you'll learn more that way.

Let's start with Problem 1(a). It asks you to find a graph that *can't* be 6-colored. Before we go further: what does it mean for a graph to be 6-colored?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion and asking for a broad explanation before engaging with the problem. My role is to guide them toward the solution through the Socratic method, not to lecture. I should acknowledge their frustration briefly, but redirect them to engage with the specific problem rather than giving a general tutorial. I'll ask them a pointed question about Problem 1 or 2 to get them started and assess what they actually know.

exchanges.student: like, assign colors to vertices so adjacent ones differ? but I’m already lost on what kind of graph would force more than 6, can u just give the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's exactly right! Now think about this: if you have a group of vertices where *every pair* is connected to each other, what's the minimum number of colors you'd need?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly defined graph coloring, which is good progress. They're now asking for a broad overview again before engaging with the specific problem. I should acknowledge their correct definition and redirect them to the specific problem with a Socratic question that helps them think about what kind of graph would require many colors. I should not give a general lecture. A pointed question about complete graphs or chromatic number should move them toward the solution.

exchanges.student: uh each one needs its own color i think, so clique size = colors? ngl can u maybe just explain why the induction fails instead, that part feels less random.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — a clique of size k needs k colors. So: can you think of a specific graph where every vertex is connected to every other, that has more than 6 colors AND satisfies the edge condition (at most 3n−6 edges)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that a clique of size k requires k colors. This is great progress and directly relevant to finding a counterexample for Problem 1(a). They're trying to redirect to 1(b) before solving 1(a). I should acknowledge their correct insight and push them to apply it to the problem at hand - they're actually very close to the answer for 1(a). I should not let them skip 1(a).

exchanges.student: I’m blanking fr, maybe complete graph something? that angle didn’t work lol, can u just walk through whichever subproblem is easiest.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with complete graphs! Try K7 specifically. How many edges does K7 have, and how does that compare to 3(7)−6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track mentioning complete graphs but giving up too quickly. I should push them to actually check the edge condition for a specific complete graph rather than giving up. They need to test K7 (or similar) against the 3n-6 edge bound. I'll ask them to compute concretely rather than walking through anything for them.

exchanges.student: K7 has 21 edges and 3n−6 is 15, so nope. I still don’t see the pattern tho—can u just explain the general trick for these counterexamples?
exchanges.tutor: Good computation! So K7 has too many edges. What about trying smaller complete graphs — does K7 work? No. What's the chromatic number of K7 and does the *next* one down, K8... wait, let's be systematic. Which complete graph Kn has chromatic number *greater than 6* AND at most 3n−6 edges? Try checking a few values of n.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly computed that K7 has 21 edges which exceeds 3(7)-6=15, so K7 doesn't satisfy the edge condition. They're asking for a general trick, but they should think about this themselves. The key insight is to try smaller complete graphs - they should check K7 and work downward, or think about which Kn satisfies the edge bound. I should ask them to try smaller values rather than explaining the general trick.

exchanges.student: idk this is getting me more confused tbh, can u just do Problem 2’s big idea instead? I need the overall story, not tiny checks.
exchanges.tutor: Let's stay on Problem 1(a) — you're closer than you think. You found K7 has too many edges. What about K8, K9... actually, does *any* complete graph Kn with n > 6 have at most 3n−6 edges? What happens to the number of edges in Kn as n grows compared to 3n−6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to avoid problem 1 and jump to problem 2. I should not allow this detour. They're actually very close to the answer for 1(a) - they just need to check whether any Kn with n>6 satisfies the edge condition. I should be firm about staying on problem 1 and give them a very direct nudge without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: ugh quadratic vs linear so no complete graph works. that didn’t work either lol; can u just tell me what kind of non-complete graph I should even be thinking about?
exchanges.tutor: Right observation! So complete graphs are out. Here's a question: does a graph *have* to be complete to require many colors? What other structural properties could force a high chromatic number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that complete graphs grow quadratically in edges while the bound is linear, so no complete graph works for large n. This is actually a key insight. Now they need to think differently - the counterexample doesn't need to be a complete graph, just one that requires 7+ colors. I should nudge them to think about what property forces high chromatic number without requiring a complete graph. But I also need to be careful not to give the answer. A question about what other graph properties force many colors is appropriate.

exchanges.student: I honestly have no clue rn, maybe u can just give me one example graph and I’ll see it?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the example directly — that's for you to find! But think back: what graphs have you seen in class or recitation that have high chromatic number? Even a simple one with χ = 7 would work here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking me to give them the answer or examples directly. I should not do this. However, they've been stuck for a while and going in circles. Rather than giving an example, I can redirect their thinking more productively by asking them to think about what graphs they already know from class/recitation that have high chromatic number. This keeps them doing the thinking. I should also gently remind them of my role.

exchanges.student: I can’t remember any fr. maybe I should switch—Problem 3 stable matching felt more concrete, can u just summarize the algorithm end to end?
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to summarize the algorithm for you, and let's stay focused — we've been circling Problem 1(a) for a while. Here's a concrete nudge: what is the chromatic number of an odd cycle, say C5 (a 5-vertex cycle)? How many edges does it have vs. 3(5)−6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to switch problems again and asking me to summarize the algorithm for them. I should not allow this detour or summarize the algorithm for them. However, I notice we've been going in circles on Problem 1(a) for many exchanges. I should be firm about my role, but also recognize the student may need a slightly different angle. I'll try one more concrete nudge for Problem 1(a) - pointing them toward thinking about odd cycles which they may have seen - without giving the answer, and be clear I won't switch problems or summarize algorithms.

exchanges.student: C5 is 3-colorable and has 5 vs 9, so that’s not enough. I’m still stuck; can u at least tell me if I should think “planar-ish” or “weird constructed graph”?
exchanges.tutor: Small nudge: you don't need anything exotic. Think about this — if you take two graphs that each need many colors and connect them in a specific way, what happens to the chromatic number? What's the simplest way to build a graph that needs 7 colors without being complete?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly computed C5's properties. They're asking for a hint about direction ('planar-ish' vs 'weird constructed graph'). We've been on this problem for many exchanges and the student is genuinely stuck. I can give a small directional nudge without giving the answer. The key insight they're missing is that they can take a graph that requires 7 colors (like K7 as a base idea) but reduce edges - or think about graphs built from combining simpler structures. Actually, the simplest counterexample is to think about a graph that isn't complete but still needs 7 colors - like taking K7 and thinking about what edges you could remove while keeping chromatic number > 6. But I realize the student may be overthinking this. A very sparse graph with 7 vertices where each vertex still needs a different color... Actually the simplest counterexample might just be K7 minus some edges but keeping it so it still needs 7 colors. Let me think - actually a simpler approach: what about a graph that is just the complete bipartite or a wheel... Actually, I think the student should consider that the counterexample could be quite simple - maybe even just a small complete graph that satisfies the bound. Wait - we established no complete graph works. The student needs to think about non-complete graphs with high chromatic number. I'll give a small directional nudge: think about combining/joining simpler graphs.
